retrieved_docs.title: Folic Acid -- Administration -- Adult Dosing
retrieved_docs.content: The World Health Organization recommends a daily dose of 400 to 800 mcg to prevent neural tube defects in pregnancy. Clinicians generally prescribe iron-folic acid supplements for prenatal vitamins during and before pregnancy. [15] [16] Most of these include 1 mg of folate, which is more than enough to meet this criterion. [17] Again, for maximum effect, this supplementation must begin in the earliest stages of pregnancy, if not months before conception.

retrieved_docs.title: Folic Acid -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: One of the beneficial roles of folate appears to be its ability to reduce homocysteine levels in neural tube defects. [4] The period of greatest vulnerability is during the fourth week of development, when a woman may be unaware that she is pregnant. For this reason, women of childbearing age should take folic acid supplements if they are sexually active, especially when planning to conceive. If the pregnant mother were to take 4 mg of folic acid daily, it could take 20 weeks for her body to reach optimal folate levels to reduce the risk of a neural tube defect. Because of this, supplementation should be initiated 5 to 6 months before conception. [5] Adequate folic acid is also associated with a decreased risk of preterm birth. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Folic Acid Deficiency -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnant women with folate deficiency are typically advised to take 500 μg to 5 mg of folic acid 3 times daily. Women of reproductive age should take at least 0.4 mg of folic acid daily, starting before conception and continuing throughout pregnancy. High-risk women, including those with a history of neural tube defect–affected pregnancies or those taking anticonvulsants, are recommended to receive 4 to 5 mg daily during this period. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Folic Acid -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Folic acid (vitamin B9) is a water-soluble vitamin used to manage and treat megaloblastic anemia. Folic acid has FDA approval for treating megaloblastic and macrocytic anemias due to folic deficiency. This activity describes the indications, mechanism of action, and contraindications for folic acid as a valuable agent in managing megaloblastic anemia and preventing other disorders. One of the beneficial roles of folate appears to be its ability to reduce homocysteine levels in neural tube defects. In addition, this activity will highlight the adverse event profile and other key factors (eg, off-label uses, dosing, monitoring, relevant interactions) pertinent to healthcare teams dealing with conditions that can benefit from supplemental folic acid.

retrieved_docs.title: Folic Acid Deficiency -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Folic acid—the synthetic form of folate—is widely used in fortified foods and supplements due to its superior bioavailability. Given its crucial role in fetal development, especially in preventing neural tube defects, many countries, including the United States, mandate folic acid fortification of grain products as a public health measure. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Folic Acid -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Folic Acid -- Administration -- Adult Dosing
retrieved_docs.content: Patients treated with methotrexate should be prescribed folic acid supplements to reduce the adverse events associated with methotrexate therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Folic Acid -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify appropriate indications for folic acid supplementation based on patient characteristics and medical conditions. Screen patients at risk of folate deficiency, such as those with chronic alcoholism, malnutrition, or specific medical conditions. Assess patients' folate status through appropriate laboratory tests and interpret the results to guide folic acid therapy. Communicate effectively with patients, providing education about the benefits, potential side effects, and importance of adherence to folic acid therapy. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Neural Tube Disorders(Archived) -- Treatment / Management -- Folic Acid Supplementation
retrieved_docs.content: Many studies suggested that periconceptional folic acid supplementation with the fortification of a diet rich in folic acid has significant results (50% to 70%) in preventing neural tube disorders. The recommended dose of folic acid to all women desiring pregnancy is 0.4 to 0.8 mg per day. [22] However, women who already have had a child with NTD or positive family history should take 4 mg of folic acid daily one month prior to the conception through the first three months of pregnancy to prevent the recurrence. Some studies suggest that it takes approximately 20 weeks to achieve a normal red-blood-cell level of folic acid to prevent neural tube defects. [23] So women should receive folic acid 5 to 6 months before conception.

retrieved_docs.title: Folic Acid Deficiency -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify key biological functions of folate and clinical manifestations of folic acid deficiency, including hematological, neuropsychiatric, and reproductive health impacts. Implement appropriate diagnostic evaluations, including serum folate, red blood cell folate, homocysteine, and methylmalonic acid testing. Apply evidence-based supplementation strategies tailored to the severity of deficiency and patient-specific risk factors. Collaborate with interprofessional healthcare team members to improve communication, coordinate care, and develop comprehensive management plans for patients at risk for folic acid deficiency. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Folic Acid Deficiency -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: With appropriate treatment, the prognosis for folic acid deficiency is favorable, as most clinical and biochemical abnormalities are reversible. Folate supplementation typically raises serum folate levels within 17 days and can reduce the risk of megaloblastic anemia by up to 79%.

retrieved_docs.title: Folic Acid Deficiency -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Key facts to keep in mind about folic acid deficiency include:

retrieved_docs.title: Folic Acid Deficiency -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Women of childbearing age are strongly encouraged to consume folate-rich foods and take at least 0.4 mg of folic acid daily, starting before conception and continuing throughout pregnancy, to reduce the risk of neural tube defects and other pregnancy-related complications. Women at higher risk, such as those with a history of neural tube defect–affected pregnancies or those taking anticonvulsants, should take higher doses (4–5 mg/d) under medical supervision. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Folic Acid -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: RDA for supplementation during pregnancy is 600 mcg orally daily. There is no known risk of fetal harm. RDA for supplementation during pregnancy is 500 mcg orally daily. There is no known risk of infant harm or adverse effects on milk production.

retrieved_docs.title: Folic Acid -- Administration -- Adult Dosing
retrieved_docs.content: Folic acid is often administered as an oral supplement. Dosing is usually dependent on the disorder. The recommended daily requirement of folic acid for an adult is 400 mcg. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2029
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIANEMIC PREPARATIONS are classified as vitamin B12 and folic‑acid supplements; they contain both vitamin B12 and folic acid and are used to treat or prevent anemia that results from deficiencies of these essential nutrients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1969
retrieved_docs.content: The oral tablet labeled “folic acid 1 MG / vitamin B12 0.5 MG” contains the two active ingredients vitamin B12 and folic acid, providing a combined supplement of these essential nutrients.

By incorporating folic acid into multivitamin‑iron supplements, clinicians aim to correct or avert folate deficiency, support normal erythropoiesis, and improve overall hematologic health while simultaneously providing other essential vitamins (A, C, D, E, B‑complex) and minerals (iron, calcium, magnesium, zinc, copper, iodine) that contribute to broader metabolic and physiological functions.

Deficiency of folic acid impairs DNA synthesis in rapidly dividing cells, leading to the production of abnormally large red blood cells and resulting in this type of anemia. By incorporating folic acid into multivitamin‑iron supplements, clinicians aim to correct or avert folate deficiency, support normal erythropoiesis, and improve overall hematologic health

Commercial examples illustrate the diversity of iron‑containing multivitamins: bariatric multivitamins w/ iron chewable tablets; “multivitamin with iron Multiple Vitamins with Folic Acid and Iron” tablets; “Vitamin B Complex with C, Folic Acid and Iron” capsules and tablets (including versions with docusate, probiotics, essential fatty acids or extended‑release vitamin C); liquid “Vitamin B/Ferric Pyrophosphate 100 mg/FA 0.25 mg”; and delayed‑release capsules such as Leucovorin 2.5 mg / Folic Acid 1 mg / Levomefolate Magnesium 7 mg / Ferrous Cysteine Glycinate 13.6 mg. All consistently list folic acid, underscoring its synergistic role with iron in erythropoiesis and the prevention of macrocytic (megaloblastic) anemia. The therapeutic strategy of pairing iron with folic acid is reflected in classification subgroups “iron in combination with folic acid” and “iron in other

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 49
retrieved_docs.content: anemia (Arabic: فقر دم ضَخم الأرومات), reflecting the well‑established relationship that folate deficiency can cause this anemia. By delivering iron together with folic acid and a broad spectrum of vitamins, minerals, fatty acids, and supportive agents, IRON/MULTIVITAMINS aim to correct iron‑deficiency anemia, prevent or treat folate‑deficiency‑related macrocytic anemia, and address overall nutritional needs, thereby providing a comprehensive micronutrient therapy for patients at risk of hematologic and nutritional disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1863
retrieved_docs.content: IRON/MULTIVITAMINS is a multivitamin formulation that incorporates iron and a broad spectrum of vitamins, minerals, and other nutrients to support overall health. The product is offered in several formats, including a Multi‑Day pack that adds calcium and extra iron, as well as oral capsule preparations marketed as Multivitamins w/ Iron. Its active ingredients are detailed in multiple ingredient listings: a vitamin B complex combined with vitamin C, folic acid, and iron (available as both tablets and capsules); a vitamin B complex plus vitamin C, folic acid, iron, and probiotics in capsule form; a vitamin B complex with folic acid, iron, and essential fatty acids in capsules; vitamin B12 paired with vitamin C, folic acid, and iron in capsules; vitamin B12 with folic acid and iron in capsules; and a liquid preparation that blends vitamins A, D, and C with iron. Additionally, a specific liquid ingredient, VITAMIN B/FERRIC PYROPHOSPHATE 100 MG/FA 0.25 MG, is included, providing a bioavailable source of iron. Classified as a “multivitamin agent,” IRON/MULTIVITAMINS serves as the active ingredient in these various formulations, delivering iron alongside nutrients that support hematopoiesis (folic acid, vitamin B12), antioxidant protection (vitamin C), bone health (calcium, vitamin D), and cellular function (essential fatty acids and probiotics). This integrated composition is intended to address iron deficiency while simultaneously supplying complementary vitamins and minerals essential for comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1521
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIANEMIC PREPARATIONS are classified as formulations that contain vitamin B12 and folic acid, and they are specifically described as folic acid / vitamin B12 preparations. Clinical assessment of the effectiveness and adequacy of these anti‑anemic agents involves measuring vitamin B12 and folic acid levels, which are reported as “叶酸+氰钴胺素:印象:时间点:血清/全血:名义型” – a laboratory notation indicating the impression, time point, and specimen type (serum or whole blood) for the nominal values of folic acid (叶酸) and cyanocobalamin (氰钴胺素, vitamin B12). This integrated understanding links the composition of anti‑anemic preparations with the standardized laboratory measures used to monitor patient response and guide therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B1, plain is recognized as a form of vitamin B1 that may be administered alone or together with vitamins B6 and B12, indicating that it can be used both as a single‑nutrient supplement and as part of combination vitamin preparations.

Folic acid is a key active component in many of these products—e.g., FA 1 MG/IRON (FEFM‑FEPOL) 106 MG MULTIVITAMINS CAP, delayed‑release LEUCOVORIN 2.5 MG/folic acid 1 MG/levomefolate magnesium 7 MG/ferrous cysteine glycinate 13.6 MG, VITAMIN B COMPLEX W/C & IRON, and VITAMIN B/FERRIC PYROPHOSPHATE 100 MG/FA 0.25 MG LIQUID. Its inclusion addresses the essential role of folate in DNA synthesis and red‑cell maturation; deficiency leads to macro‑cytic anemia, making combined iron‑folate multivitamins clinically important for preventing anemia and supporting hematopoiesis. Additional multivitamin‑with‑iron products that list folic acid include “Multiple Vitamins with Folic Acid and Iron,” “Vitamin B Complex with C, Folic Acid and Iron” (tablet, capsule, and versions with docusate, probiotics, or essential fatty acids), “Vitamin B12 with C, Folic Acid and Iron,” and “Vitamin C with Folic Acid and Iron” (extended‑release). Clinically, these iron‑containing multivitamins aim to prevent and treat iron‑deficiency anemia and folate‑deficiency (macrocytic) anemia, support hematopoiesis, enhance red‑blood‑cell production, and provide DNA synthesis and overall metabolic support. The inclusion of folic acid specifically addresses macrocytic anemia, while iron repletion corrects microcytic iron‑deficiency anemia. In bariatric patients, the “BARIATRIC MULTIVITAMINS W/IRON TAB, CHEWABLE” addresses heightened post‑surgical micronutrient needs. Collectively, the IRON/MULTIVITAMINS category delivers comprehensive micronutrient support for diverse patient
